messages.content: The mass of fish A is 16.5 kg and the mass of fish B is 5.48 kg. If fish A is initially swimming to the right at a speed of 15.8 m/s and fish B is at rest, after fish A swallows fish B he will be moving at how many m/s

To determine the speed of fish A after swallowing fish B while both are swimming, we can use the principle of conservation of momentum. According to this principle, if no external forces are acting on the system, the total momentum before the interaction (swallowing) must be equal to the total momentum afterward.

Let's define the direction to the right as positive. Before the swallowing occurs, fish A is moving to the right with a speed of 15.8 m/s and fish B is at rest. The total momentum before the event can be calculated as follows:

Total initial momentum (p_initial) = momentum of fish A + momentum of fish B
p_initial = (mass of fish A * velocity of fish A) + (mass of fish B * velocity of fish B)

Since fish B is at rest, its momentum is 0. So we have:
p_initial = (16.5 kg * 15.8 m/s) + (5.48 kg * 0 m/s)
p_initial = 260.7 kg*m/s + 0
p_initial = 260.7 kg*m/s

After fish A swallows fish B, they will move together as a single entity. The combined mass of fish A and fish B will be:

mass_total = mass of fish A + mass of fish B
mass_total = 16.5 kg + 5.48 kg
mass_total = 21.98 kg

The total momentum after the event (p_final) must equal the total initial momentum, so:

p_final = p_initial
(mass_total * velocity_final) = 260.7 kg*m/s

Now, solve for velocity_final:

velocity_final = p_initial / mass_total
velocity_final = 260.7 kg*m/s / 21.98 kg
velocity_final = 11.8598680609182 m/s

The final speed can be approximated to two decimal places:

velocity_final ≈ 11.86 m/s

After swallowing fish B, fish A will be moving to the right at approximately 11.86 m/s.

The mass of fish A is 11.7 kg and the mass of fish B is 9.47 kg. If fish A is initially swimming to the right at a speed of 15.2 m/s and fish B is swimming to the right at 13.05 m/s, after fish A swallows fish B he will be moving at how many m/s

To determine the speed of fish A after swallowing fish B when both are moving, we again apply the principle of conservation of momentum. Since they are both moving in the same direction (to the right), we use the same positive direction for their velocities.

Before the event, the total momentum of the system (p_initial) is the sum of the momenta of fish A and B.

\[ p_{initial} = (mass_{A} \times velocity_{A}) + (mass_{B} \times velocity_{B}) \]

Where mass_A is the mass of fish A, velocity_A is its initial velocity, mass_B is the mass of fish B, and velocity_B is its initial velocity. Plugging in the given values:

\[ p_{initial} = (11.7 \text{ kg} \times 15.2 \text{ m/s}) + (9.47 \text{ kg} \times 13.05 \text{ m/s}) \]
\[ p_{initial} = 177.84 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} + 123.5845 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \]
\[ p_{initial} = 301.4245 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \]

After fish A swallows fish B, their combined mass is:

\[ mass_{total} = mass_{A} + mass_{B} \]
\[ mass_{total} = 11.7 \text{ kg} + 9.47 \text{ kg} \]
\[ mass_{total} = 21.17 \text{ kg} \]

The total momentum after the event must be equal to the total momentum before the event, so:

\[ p_{final} = p_{initial} \]
\[ mass_{total} \times velocity_{final} = p_{initial} \]

Now we can solve for the velocity_final:

\[ velocity_{final} = p_{initial} / mass_{total} \]
\[ velocity_{final} = 301.4245 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} / 21.17 \text{ kg} \]
\[ velocity_{final} ≈ 14.24 \text{ m/s} \]

The speed of fish A after it has swallowed fish B is approximately 14.24 m/s to the right.

The mass of fish A is 12.4 kg and the mass of fish C is 1.06 kg. If fish A is initially swimming to the right at a speed of 19.4 m/s and fish C is swimming to the left at 6.71 m/s, after a fish A swallows fish C he will be moving at how many m/s

To determine the speed of fish A after swallowing fish C when they are moving in opposite directions, we'll again use the principle of conservation of momentum. Before the swallowing happens, the total momentum of the system is the sum of the individual momenta of fish A and fish C. When they are moving in opposite directions, one will have positive momentum and the other negative.

Let's take the direction to the right as positive. This means fish C's momentum will be negative, since it's moving to the left.

Before the event, the total momentum of the system (p_initial) is:

\[ p_{initial} = (mass_{A} \times velocity_{A}) + (mass_{C} \times (-velocity_{C})) \]

\[ p_{initial} = (12.4 \text{ kg} \times 19.4 \text{ m/s}) - (1.06 \text{ kg} \times 6.71 \text{ m/s}) \]

\[ p_{initial} = (240.36 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}) - (7.1126 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}) \]

\[ p_{initial} = 240.36 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} - 7.1126 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \]

\[ p_{initial} \approx 233.2474 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \]

After fish A swallows fish C, their combined mass is:

\[ mass_{total} = mass_{A} + mass_{C} \]

\[ mass_{total} = 12.4 \text{ kg} + 1.06 \text{ kg} \]

\[ mass_{total} = 13.46 \text{ kg} \]

Without any external forces, after the swallowing event, the total momentum (p_final) equals the initial momentum, so we have:

\[ p_{final} = mass_{total} \times velocity_{final} \]

\[ velocity_{final} = p_{initial} / mass_{total} \]

\[ velocity_{final} = 233.2474 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} / 13.46 \text{ kg} \]

\[ velocity_{final} \approx 17.333341 \text{ m/s} \]

When rounding to two decimal places, fish A's speed after swallowing fish C is approximately:

\[ velocity_{final} \approx 17.33 \text{ m/s} \]

Therefore, after fish A swallows fish C, it will be moving to the right at approximately 17.33 m/s.

The two gliders in the picture above have the same mass. They are free to slide from side to side on a cushion of air, and thus experience almost no friction. The glider on the left collides with and sticks to the glider on the right. Vo is equal to 16 m/s. After the collision, the gliders will move at how many m/s

The scenario you described is an example of a perfectly inelastic collision in which two objects collide and then move together as a single entity. Since you mentioned that the gliders have the same mass and that the one on the right is stationary while the one on the left is moving with an initial velocity (V₀) of 16 m/s, we can use the conservation of momentum principle to determine the final velocity of the two-gliders system after the collision.

Let \( m \) be the mass of each glider, \( V₀ \) be the initial velocity of the moving glider, and \( u \) be the final velocity of both gliders together after the collision.

Before the collision, the total momentum of the system \( p_{\text{initial}} \) is just the momentum of the moving glider because the second glider is stationary. After the collision, both gliders stick together, and their combined mass is \( 2m \), and they move with a common velocity \( u \).

Since no external forces are acting on the system, the momentum is conserved:

\[ p_{\text{initial}} = p_{\text{final}} \]

Initially, only the left glider has momentum:

\[ m \times V₀ = p_{\text{initial}} \]

The glider on the right is at rest, so its initial momentum is zero.

The final momentum is the total mass of both gliders moving together at their final velocity \( u \):

\[ 2m \times u = p_{\text{final}} \]

Since momentum is conserved:

\[ m \times V₀ = 2m \times u \]

Now we can solve for \( u \):

\[ V₀ = 2u \]

\[ u = \frac{V₀}{2} \]

\[ u = \frac{16 \text{ m/s}}{2} \]

\[ u = 8 \text{ m/s} \]

After the collision, the two-gliders system will move together at a velocity of 8 m/s.

The mass of the player in the red shirt is 65.2 kg and the player in the blue shirt is 65 kg. While attempting to tackle the player in the red shirt, the player in blue grabs and holds onto the player in red. The velocity of the two players immediately after the collision is how many m/s

If the players in red and blue are engaging in a tackle and just at the moment they grab onto each other you're interested in their velocity, we will need the initial velocities of both players before the collision. 

However, you haven't provided the initial velocities for either player, which are necessary to calculate the combined velocity of the players immediately after the collision.

Conservation of momentum dictates that the total momentum before the collision is equal to the total momentum after the collision, assuming no external forces are involved. The formula for total momentum before the collision is:

\[ p_{\text{initial}} = m_{\text{red}} \cdot v_{\text{red}} + m_{\text{blue}} \cdot v_{\text{blue}} \]

where \( m_{\text{red}} \) is the mass of the player in the red shirt, \( m_{\text{blue}} \) is the mass of the player in the blue shirt, \( v_{\text{red}} \) is the velocity of the player in the red shirt, and \( v_{\text{blue}} \) is the velocity of the player in the blue shirt.

After the collision, since the two players grab onto each other, they effectively become a single entity with a combined mass of \( m_{\text{red}} + m_{\text{blue}} \), and they move together with a velocity \( v_{\text{final}} \). Thus, for the total momentum after the collision, we have:

\[ p_{\text{final}} = (m_{\text{red}} + m_{\text{blue}}) \cdot v_{\text{final}} \]

For conservation of momentum:

\[ p_{\text{initial}} = p_{\text{final}} \]

If we fill in the known masses and calculate with the velocities, we can find the final velocity, but without the initial velocities provided, we cannot compute the final velocity of the two players immediately after the collision. If you provide the initial velocities of both players, I would be able to assist you further.
3.75 m/s for the player going right and 4.10 for the player going left

Given that the player in the red shirt (moving to the right) has a mass of 65.2 kg and an initial velocity of 3.75 m/s, and the player in the blue shirt (moving to the left) has a mass of 65 kg and an initial velocity of 4.10 m/s, we can use the principle of conservation of momentum to find the velocity of the two players immediately after the collision.

Momentum is a vector quantity, meaning direction is important. We will define the direction to the right as positive and the direction to the left as negative.

Before the collision, the total momentum of the system \( p_{\text{initial}} \) is the sum of the momenta of the individual players:

\[ p_{\text{initial}} = m_{\text{red}} \cdot v_{\text{red}} + m_{\text{blue}} \cdot (-v_{\text{blue}}) \]
\[ p_{\text{initial}} = (65.2 \, \text{kg} \cdot 3.75 \, \text{m/s}) + (65 \, \text{kg} \cdot -4.10 \, \text{m/s}) \]
\[ p_{\text{initial}} = 244.5 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s} - 266.5 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \]
\[ p_{\text{initial}} = -22 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \]

This negative result indicates that the initial momentum of the system is towards the left.

After the collision, the players grab onto each other and move together with a combined mass of \( m_{\text{total}} \) and a combined velocity \( v_{\text{final}} \):

\[ m_{\text{total}} = m_{\text{red}} + m_{\text{blue}} \]
\[ m_{\text{total}} = 65.2 \, \text{kg} + 65 \, \text{kg} \]
\[ m_{\text{total}} = 130.2 \, \text{kg} \]

The conservation of momentum principle tells us:

\[ p_{\text{final}} = p_{\text{initial}} \]
\[ m_{\text{total}} \cdot v_{\text{final}} = -22 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \]

Solving for \( v_{\text{final}} \):

\[ v_{\text{final}} = \frac{-22 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}}{130.2 \text{ kg}} \]
\[ v_{\text{final}} \approx -0.169 \text{ m/s} \]

The negative sign indicates that the direction of the velocity is to the left.

Therefore, the two players combined will be moving to the left at approximately 0.169 m/s immediately after the collision.

The lumberjack and the log are initially at rest. The lumberjack begins to run to the right at a speed of 2.70 m/s relative to the water. If the mass of the lumberjack is 95.3 kg and the mass of the log is 95.5 kg, the speed with which the log will move to the left is how many m/s.

The scenario described is an example of conservation of momentum where no external horizontal forces are acting on the system. Initially, both the lumberjack and the log are at rest, meaning the system's total initial momentum is zero. When the lumberjack starts running to the right, the log will move in the opposite direction due to the conservation of momentum.

Since the initial momentum of the system is zero, the momentum must remain zero after the lumberjack starts moving. If we take the direction to the right as positive, then the momentum to the left will be negative.

Let \( v_{\text{lumberjack}} \) represent the speed of the lumberjack, \( m_{\text{lumberjack}} \) be the mass of the lumberjack, \( v_{\text{log}} \) be the speed of the log, and \( m_{\text{log}} \) be the mass of the log.

Given:
- \( m_{\text{lumberjack}} = 95.3 \) kg
- \( v_{\text{lumberjack}} = 2.70 \) m/s
- \( m_{\text{log}} = 95.5 \) kg

Using the law of conservation of momentum:

\[ m_{\text{lumberjack}} \times v_{\text{lumberjack}} + m_{\text{log}} \times (-v_{\text{log}}) = 0 \]

The negative sign in front of \( v_{\text{log}} \) accounts for the fact that the log moves in the opposite direction. We can now solve for \( v_{\text{log}} \):

\[ 95.3 \times 2.70 + 95.5 \times (-v_{\text{log}}) = 0 \]

\[ 257.31 - 95.5 \times v_{\text{log}} = 0 \]

\[ 95.5 \times v_{\text{log}} = 257.31 \]

\[ v_{\text{log}} = \frac{257.31}{95.5} \]

\[ v_{\text{log}} = 2.694 \text{ m/s} \]

Therefore, the log will move to the left at approximately \( 2.694 \) m/s. Given that the two masses are nearly identical, it makes sense that the speeds are also nearly identical, and any small discrepancies can come from rounding differences in the problem statement or solution calculations. However, given the masses provided, this is the precise calculation.

The mass of fish A is 16.4 kg and the mass of fish C is 1.38 kg. If fish A is initially swimming to the right at a speed of i 6.3 m/s and fish C is swimming to the right at 5.54 m/s, after fish A swallows fish C he will be moving at how many m/s.

To find the velocity of fish A after it swallows fish C, we apply the principle of conservation of momentum. We're told that both fish A and fish C are swimming to the right, so their velocities will be added together. 

Since fish A swallows fish C, the two fish will form a single entity after the collision, and their total momentum before and after the collision should be the same, assuming no external forces are acting on the system and there is no loss of momentum during the process.

Let's define fishes' momentums, starting with fish A:
\( p_{A} = m_{A} \times v_{A} \)
and fish C:
\( p_{C} = m_{C} \times v_{C} \)

Where:
- \( m_{A} \) is the mass of fish A = 16.4 kg
- \( v_{A} \) is the initial velocity of fish A = 6.3 m/s
- \( m_{C} \) is the mass of fish C = 1.38 kg
- \( v_{C} \) is the initial velocity of fish C = 5.54 m/s

Calculate the individual momentums:
\( p_{A} = 16.4 \text{ kg} \times 6.3 \text{ m/s} = 103.32 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \)
\( p_{C} = 1.38 \text{ kg} \times 5.54 \text{ m/s} = 7.6452 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \)

The total initial momentum of the system is the sum of the individual momentums:
\( p_{\text{total initial}} = p_{A} + p_{C} = 103.32 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} + 7.6452 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \)
\( p_{\text{total initial}} = 110.9652 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s} \)

After the collision, the combined mass of fish A and C is:
\( m_{\text{total}} = m_{A} + m_{C} = 16.4 \text{ kg} + 1.38 \text{ kg} = 17.78 \text{ kg} \)

The total final momentum should equal the total initial momentum:
\( p_{\text{total initial}} = p_{\text{total final}} \)
\( m_{\text{total}} \times v_{\text{final}} = p_{\text{total initial}} \)

Solving for the final velocity:
\( v_{\text{final}} = \frac{p_{\text{total initial}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \)
\( v_{\text{final}} = \frac{110.9652 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}}{17.78 \text{ kg}} \)
\( v_{\text{final}} = 6.2406952 \text{ m/s} \)

So the velocity of fish A after it swallows fish C is approximately 6.24 m/s to the right.
The mass of the player in the red shirt is 60.8 kg. When the player in blue tackles the player in red, they both come to rest. The mass of the player in blue is how many kg.

In the scenario described, the two players come to rest after the player in blue tackles the player in red. This means that the total momentum of the two-player system after the tackle is zero because they are not moving.

Since no velocities are described before the tackle, we are missing information to determine the mass of the player in blue based on conservation of momentum. To apply the conservation of momentum, we need the initial velocities of the players before the collision.

The principle of conservation of momentum states that the total momentum before the collision equals the total momentum after the collision. This can be expressed as:

\[ m_{\text{red}} \cdot v_{\text{red}} + m_{\text{blue}} \cdot v_{\text{blue}} = (m_{\text{red}} + m_{\text{blue}}) \cdot v_{\text{final}} \]

Where:
- \( m_{\text{red}} \) is the mass of the red player (60.8 kg).
- \( v_{\text{red}} \) is the velocity of the red player before the collision.
- \( m_{\text{blue}} \) is the mass of the blue player (unknown).
- \( v_{\text{blue}} \) is the velocity of the blue player before the collision.
- \( v_{\text{final}} \) is the final velocity of both players after the collision (0 m/s).

Since the final velocity is zero, whatever the initial momenta of the two players were, they would cancel out to give a final momentum of zero. This doesn't provide any information to solve for \( m_{\text{blue}} \) without knowing at least one initial velocity. If either of the initial velocities was provided, it would be possible to calculate \( m_{\text{blue}} \).

If additional information can be supplied regarding the velocities of the players before the tackle, the mass of the player in the blue shirt can be calculated. Please provide either the velocity of one or both players before the collision to proceed.
The red player is going 3.75 m/s and the blue player is going -4.10 m/s

With the additional information provided, we can indeed use conservation of momentum to find the mass of the player in blue. 

According to the conservation of momentum, the total momentum before the collision is equal to the total momentum after the collision. In this case, after the collision, the momentum is zero, because both players come to rest. 

Let's denote the mass of the red player (moving right) as \( m_{\text{red}} \), the initial velocity of the red player as \( v_{\text{red}} \), the mass of the blue player (moving left) as \( m_{\text{blue}} \), and the initial velocity of the blue player as \( v_{\text{blue}} \).

The given values are:
- \( m_{\text{red}} = 60.8 \) kg
- \( v_{\text{red}} = 3.75 \) m/s (since it's to the right, it's positive)
- \( v_{\text{blue}} = -4.10 \) m/s (since it's to the left, it's negative)

The total momentum before the collision (\( p_{\text{total before}} \)) is:

\[ p_{\text{total before}} = m_{\text{red}} \cdot v_{\text{red}} + m_{\text{blue}} \cdot v_{\text{blue}} \]

After the collision, because both players come to rest, the total momentum (\( p_{\text{total after}} \)) is 0.

Using conservation of momentum:

\[ p_{\text{total before}} = p_{\text{total after}} \]

\[ m_{\text{red}} \cdot v_{\text{red}} + m_{\text{blue}} \cdot v_{\text{blue}} = 0 \]

Now we insert the given values and solve for \( m_{\text{blue}} \):

\[ 60.8 \, \text{kg} \cdot 3.75 \, \text{m/s} + m_{\text{blue}} \cdot (-4.10 \, \text{m/s}) = 0 \]

\[ 228 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s} = m_{\text{blue}} \cdot 4.10 \, \text{m/s} \]

\[ m_{\text{blue}} = \frac{228}{4.10} \]

\[ m_{\text{blue}} \approx 55.61 \, \text{kg} \]

Therefore, based on conservation of momentum, the mass of the blue player would be approximately \( 55.61 \) kg.

An astronaut, who is initially at rest, catches a book with a mass of 2.10 kg that is thrown toward him at 2.28 m/s, as pictured above. If the astronaut and book then drift to the right with a speed of 0.075 m/s, the mass of the astronaut must be how many kg.

To find the mass of the astronaut, we'll use the principle of conservation of momentum. Before the astronaut catches the book, the astronaut is at rest, and the book is moving towards the astronaut. After catching the book, the astronaut and the book move together with a new velocity. 

Let \( m_{\text{book}} \) be the mass of the book, \( v_{\text{book}} \) be the velocity of the book before being caught, \( m_{\text{astronaut}} \) be the mass of the astronaut, \( v_{\text{astronaut}} \) be the velocity of the astronaut (which is zero since he's initially at rest), and \( v_{\text{final}} \) be the final velocity of the astronaut and the book moving together.

Given:
- \( m_{\text{book}} = 2.10 \) kg
- \( v_{\text{book}} = 2.28 \) m/s
- \( m_{\text{astronaut}} \) is unknown
- \( v_{\text{astronaut}} = 0 \) m/s (since the astronaut is initially at rest)
- \( v_{\text{final}} = 0.075 \) m/s

Using conservation of momentum, we can set up the equation:

\[ (m_{\text{astronaut}} \cdot v_{\text{astronaut}}) + (m_{\text{book}} \cdot v_{\text{book}}) = (m_{\text{astronaut}} + m_{\text{book}}) \cdot v_{\text{final}} \]

Since \( v_{\text{astronaut}} = 0 \) m/s:

\[ m_{\text{astronaut}} \cdot 0 + m_{\text{book}} \cdot v_{\text{book}} = (m_{\text{astronaut}} + m_{\text{book}}) \cdot v_{\text{final}} \]

This simplifies to:

\[ m_{\text{book}} \cdot v_{\text{book}} = m_{\text{astronaut}} \cdot v_{\text{final}} + m_{\text{book}} \cdot v_{\text{final}} \]

Rearranging the terms to solve for \( m_{\text{astronaut}} \):

\[ m_{\text{astronaut}} \cdot v_{\text{final}} = m_{\text{book}} \cdot v_{\text{book}} - m_{\text{book}} \cdot v_{\text{final}} \]

\[ m_{\text{astronaut}} = \frac{m_{\text{book}} \cdot v_{\text{book}} - m_{\text{book}} \cdot v_{\text{final}}}{v_{\text{final}} } \]

\[ m_{\text{astronaut}} = \frac{m_{\text{book}} \cdot (v_{\text{book}} - v_{\text{final}})}{v_{\text{final}} } \]

Now, plug in the numbers:

\[ m_{\text{astronaut}} = \frac{2.10 \, \text{kg} \cdot (2.28 \, \text{m/s} - 0.075 \, \text{m/s})}{0.075 \, \text{m/s}} \]

\[ m_{\text{astronaut}} = \frac{2.10 \, \text{kg} \cdot 2.205 \, \text{m/s}}{0.075 \, \text{m/s}} \]

\[ m_{\text{astronaut}} = \frac{4.6305 \, \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s}}{0.075 \, \text{m/s}} \]

\[ m_{\text{astronaut}} = 61.74 \, \text{kg} \]

Therefore, the mass of the astronaut would be approximately 61.74 kg.

A bee, whose mass is 3.31 grams, is floating on a stick. The mass of the stick is 3 times the mass of the bee. If the bee begins to walk toward the right with a speed of 1.39 cm/s relative to the water, the velocity of the stick will be how many cm/s.

To solve for the velocity of the stick, we can use the principle of conservation of momentum. Initially, both the bee and the stick are at rest relative to the water, so their combined momentum is zero. When the bee walks on the stick, it exerts a force on the stick, causing the stick to move in the opposite direction. However, the total momentum of the system must remain zero because no external horizontal forces are acting on the system.

Let's denote the mass of the bee as \( m_{\text{bee}} \) and its velocity as \( v_{\text{bee}} \), and the mass of the stick as \( m_{\text{stick}} \) and its velocity as \( v_{\text{stick}} \). 

Given:
- \( m_{\text{bee}} = 3.31 \) grams
- \( m_{\text{stick}} = 3 \times m_{\text{bee}} \)
- \( v_{\text{bee}} = 1.39 \) cm/s (since it's to the right, we'll consider it positive)

We want to find \( v_{\text{stick}} \).

According to the conservation of momentum:

\[ m_{\text{bee}} \cdot v_{\text{bee}} + m_{\text{stick}} \cdot v_{\text{stick}} = 0 \]

Since \( m_{\text{stick}} \) is 3 times \( m_{\text{bee}} \):

\[ m_{\text{bee}} \cdot v_{\text{bee}} + 3 \cdot m_{\text{bee}} \cdot v_{\text{stick}} = 0 \]

Now we can solve for \( v_{\text{stick}} \):

\[ 3.31 \cdot 1.39 + 3 \cdot 3.31 \cdot v_{\text{stick}} = 0 \]
\[ 4.6009 + 9.93 \cdot v_{\text{stick}} = 0 \]

Rearrange to solve for \( v_{\text{stick}} \):

\[ v_{\text{stick}} = -\frac{4.6009}{9.93} \]
\[ v_{\text{stick}} \approx -0.4633 \text{ cm/s} \]

Since we get a negative velocity, it means that the direction of the stick's velocity is to the left, opposite to the direction of the bee's velocity to the right. The magnitude of the stick's velocity would be approximately 0.4633 cm/s.
Two identical railcars, one of which is at rest, collide and stick together. If the final velocity of the two railcars is 4.27 m/s, the initial velocity of the railcar on the left was how many m/s.

When two identical railcars collide and stick together, and one of them is initially at rest, we can use the conservation of momentum to find the initial velocity of the railcar that was moving. 

Let's denote the mass of the moving railcar as \( m \) and its initial velocity as \( v_{\text{initial}} \). The mass of the railcar at rest is also \( m \), but its initial velocity is \( 0 \) m/s. The mass of the system after collision, when both railcars stick together, is \( 2m \), and the final velocity of both railcars together as one single object is given as \( v_{\text{final}} = 4.27 \) m/s.

Since one of the railcars was initially at rest, the total initial momentum of the system is just the momentum of the moving railcar. After the collision, the combined momentum of the two railcars is the product of their combined mass and their final velocity.

Using the conservation of momentum:

\[ p_{\text{initial}} = p_{\text{final}} \]

\[ m \cdot v_{\text{initial}} + m \cdot 0 = (2m) \cdot v_{\text{final}} \]

Notice that the mass \( m \) will cancel out on both sides of the equation since it's the same for both railcars and both terms on the left side of the equation:

\[ v_{\text{initial}} = 2 \cdot v_{\text{final}} \]

Now plug in the final velocity:

\[ v_{\text{initial}} = 2 \cdot 4.27 \text{ m/s} \]

\[ v_{\text{initial}} = 8.54 \text{ m/s} \]

Therefore, the initial velocity of the moving railcar on the left was 8.54 m/s.

A 14,000 kg grain car is rolling to the right with an initial velocity of 0.74 m/s. As the car rolls under a stationary grain dispenser, 5,000 kg of grain are loaded into the car. The speed of the car after the grain has been loaded is how many m/s.

To find the speed of the grain car after the grain has been loaded, we use the principle of conservation of momentum. The total momentum of the system is conserved since no external net forces are acting on the system.

Before the grain is loaded, the momentum of the car \( p_{\text{initial}} \) is the product of the mass of the car \( m_{\text{car}} \) and its initial velocity \( v_{\text{initial}} \). After the grain is loaded, the momentum \( p_{\text{final}} \) is the product of the total mass of the car and the grain \( m_{\text{total}} \) and the final velocity of the car \( v_{\text{final}} \).

Given:
- \( m_{\text{car}} = 14,000 \) kg (initial mass of the car)
- \( v_{\text{initial}} = 0.74 \) m/s (initial velocity of the car)
- \( m_{\text{grain}} = 5,000 \) kg (mass of the grain loaded into the car)

The total mass after loading the grain is:
- \( m_{\text{total}} = m_{\text{car}} + m_{\text{grain}} \)
- \( m_{\text{total}} = 14,000 \) kg \( + 5,000 \) kg
- \( m_{\text{total}} = 19,000 \) kg

Now apply the conservation of momentum:
- \( p_{\text{initial}} = p_{\text{final}} \)
- \( m_{\text{car}} \cdot v_{\text{initial}} = m_{\text{total}} \cdot v_{\text{final}} \)

Solving for \( v_{\text{final}} \):
- \( v_{\text{final}} = \frac{m_{\text{car}} \cdot v_{\text{initial}}}{m_{\text{total}}} \)
- \( v_{\text{final}} = \frac{14,000 \text{ kg} \cdot 0.74 \text{ m/s}}{19,000 \text{ kg}} \)
- \( v_{\text{final}} = \frac{10,360 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}}{19,000 \text{ kg}} \)
- \( v_{\text{final}} \approx 0.5453 \text{ m/s} \)

The speed of the car after the grain has been loaded is approximately 0.545 m/s.
